content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

For years, we treated mistakes like the enemy.
A typo? Fix it.
A clumsy sentence? Rewrite it.
An awkward paragraph? Polish it until every word sat perfectly in place.
That was the standard. If your writing looked clean, structured, and error-free, people assumed you knew what you were doing. Good writing was a skill. Sometimes it took hours to get a page exactly right.
Then AI showed up.
Now anyone can produce a perfectly written article in seconds. Flawless grammar. Logical structure. Smooth transitions. Nice headings. Bullet points exactly where you’d expect them.
The funny part?
None of that feels impressive anymore.
It’s just what machines do.
And I think we’re about to see something interesting happen.
When perfect writing becomes free, tiny human imperfections suddenly become valuable.
Perfect Content Is Everywhere. That’s the Problem.
Think about Instagram.
Remember when everyone wanted their photos to look perfect?
People spent ages editing selfies. Skin became unrealistically smooth. Colors were boosted until sunsets looked like they came from another planet. Every little flaw disappeared.
Then AI made perfect photos available to everyone.
And almost overnight, people got tired of them.
Suddenly film cameras became cool again.
Grain.
Blurry shots.
Crooked horizons.
Random flashes.
Photos that looked like someone actually lived the moment instead of spending twenty minutes editing it afterward.
Why?
Because imperfections feel real.
A tiny scratch on a photo tells a story. It reminds you that a person stood there holding the camera. Maybe their hands shook. Maybe the light wasn’t perfect. Maybe that moment only happened once.
That’s exactly what’s starting to happen with writing.
We Can Tell When Nobody Was Really There
Have you ever opened an article and instantly knew AI probably wrote it?
Not because it contained mistakes.
Because it didn’t.
Everything felt…too smooth.
Every paragraph had the same rhythm.
Every idea flowed perfectly into the next one.
Nothing unexpected happened.
After reading two or three paragraphs, your brain quietly checks out.
You may not even know why.
It just feels plastic.
Like listening to someone who always says the right thing but never says anything personal.
The strange thing is that AI isn’t bad at writing.
It’s actually too good at sounding average.
It predicts what people usually write, then gives you the safest possible version of it.
That makes the result easy to read.
But also easy to forget.
The Kind of “Mistakes” AI Still Can’t Copy
I’m not talking about bad grammar or spelling errors.
Nobody wants to read that.
I’m talking about the little rough edges that naturally appear when a real person is thinking while they write.
Sometimes your thoughts don’t arrive in a straight line.
You begin talking about one thing…
Then another memory pops into your head.
You follow it for a moment.
And somehow, by the end of the paragraph, you’ve reached a conclusion you didn’t even expect when you started typing.
People do that all the time.
AI almost never does.
It likes clean roads.
Humans wander.
Real People Don’t Breathe Like Machines
One thing I’ve started noticing is the rhythm.
AI writes like it’s keeping perfect time with a metronome.
Paragraph.
Paragraph.
Paragraph.
Almost the same length every time.
People don’t write like that.
Sometimes you’re trying to catch a complicated thought before it disappears, so the sentence keeps growing.
And growing.
Then suddenly…
You stop.
One short sentence.
Because that’s all that needs to be said.
It feels natural because that’s how people actually think.
Not in perfect patterns.
But in bursts.
We Read People for Their Opinions, Not Their Balance
Another giveaway is how careful AI tries to be.
It almost always presents both sides.
“This approach has advantages, but it also has disadvantages.”
Technically that’s correct.
Emotionally it’s forgettable.
Real people aren’t that balanced all the time.
Sometimes you absolutely love a tool.
Sometimes you can’t stand it because it wasted your entire afternoon.
And that’s exactly what makes someone interesting to read.
I don’t open Medium because I want another Wikipedia article.
I open it because I want to know what you think after actually using something.
That’s the part AI still struggles to fake.
Even when it imitates emotion, it usually sounds like someone acting happy instead of actually feeling it.
Weird Details Are Becoming More Valuable
AI is getting better every month.
But there’s one thing it still struggles with.
Specific memories.
Not famous stories.
Not polished examples.
The tiny, random details that only make sense because they actually happened.
Maybe you compare organizing your files to the way your grandfather kept rusty screws in old coffee jars in his garage.
Or maybe a certain AI tool reminds you of that coworker who always volunteered to help… and somehow made everything slower.
Those comparisons aren’t efficient.
They’re not optimized.
They’re just yours.
And that’s exactly why people remember them.
AI usually reaches for familiar metaphors.
A needle in a haystack.
Opening new doors.
Connecting the dots.
We’ve seen them thousands of times.
Our brains barely notice them anymore.
Real People Admit They Don’t Know Everything
Here’s another thing I’ve started appreciating.
Uncertainty.
AI almost always sounds confident.
Even when it’s wrong.
People aren’t like that.
Sometimes you’re halfway through writing an article and realize your original opinion wasn’t actually correct.
Sometimes you test something expecting one result…
…and get the exact opposite.
That happens more often than we’d like to admit.
Personally, I trust writers more when they say things like:
“I might be wrong here.”
Or…
“This surprised me because I expected something completely different.”
Or even…
“A year ago I would’ve argued the opposite.”
That doesn’t make someone look weak.
It makes them believable.
Changing your mind is one of the most human things you can do.
Tiny Flaws Can Become Proof That Someone Was Actually There
There’s an idea in cryptocurrency called Proof of Work.
Very simply, value comes from the effort that was required to produce something.
I keep wondering if writing is moving toward something similar.
Not literally, of course.
I’m not saying we should start adding mistakes on purpose.
That would be just as fake.
But maybe we’ll stop obsessing over making every sentence look as if it came out of a machine.
Think about handmade furniture.
If you look closely, you’ll notice tiny imperfections.
The edge isn’t perfectly straight.
The wood grain isn’t symmetrical.
Maybe one corner feels slightly different from the others.
Nobody complains about those things.
In fact, they’re often the reason the piece costs more.
They tell you a real person spent hours making it.
Mass production removes imperfections.
Craftsmanship leaves traces.
Writing may be heading in exactly the same direction.
A sentence that’s a little awkward.
An unusual choice of words.
A paragraph that’s slightly messy because the idea arrived faster than the editing.
Those things don’t necessarily reduce trust anymore.
Sometimes they increase it.
Because they remind you there’s a human on the other side of the screen.
I Started Editing Less, Not More
Something changed in the way I use AI.
A year ago my workflow was predictable.
I’d finish writing.
Paste everything into ChatGPT.
Then ask it to improve the style, fix mistakes, make it more professional.
The result always looked polished.
Clean.
Smooth.
Ready to publish.
At least that’s what I thought.
A few months ago I went back and reread some drafts I’d written years earlier.
They weren’t perfect.
Some paragraphs wandered a little.
I repeated certain words far too often.
A few jokes didn’t really land.
But they had personality.
They sounded like me.
Not like an excellent writer.
Just…me.
That was enough.
These days I use AI differently.
I’ll ask it to help organize ideas.
I’ll use it to spot weak arguments or find information I missed.
Sometimes it points out a logical gap I hadn’t noticed.
That’s incredibly useful.
But when the article is almost finished, I stop asking it to rewrite my voice.
I don’t want every sentence polished until it becomes interchangeable with a million others.
I’d rather leave a few rough edges behind.
The Next Signal of Trust Won’t Be Perfection
For years, quality was easy to recognize.
If something looked polished, people assumed it was written by someone who knew what they were doing.
Now that assumption doesn’t really work anymore.
AI can produce polished content in seconds.
So readers are starting to look for different signals.
Not bigger words.
Not better formatting.
Not perfect grammar.
They’re looking for signs that a real person actually thought about what they’re reading.
A surprising observation.
An opinion that isn’t trying to please everyone.
A story that feels too oddly specific to have been generated.
Even small moments of hesitation.
Ironically, those things are becoming harder to fake than perfect writing itself.
The Human Advantage Is Moving
For years we competed with machines on speed.
Then on knowledge.
Now we compete on something completely different.
Perspective.
Two people can ask ChatGPT the exact same question and get almost identical answers.
But ask those two people what they actually believe after reading those answers…
Now the conversation becomes interesting.
AI can generate information.
It can’t live your experiences.
It doesn’t know what made you change your mind three years ago.
It wasn’t there when a tiny mistake taught you a lesson you’ll never forget.
Those are the things people connect with.
Not because they’re dramatic.
Because they’re real.
Maybe the Goal Was Never to Sound Perfect
I think a lot of us are going through the same phase.
We discovered AI.
We were amazed by how clean it could write.
Then we started polishing everything.
Every sentence.
Every paragraph.
Every headline.
For a while, it felt like progress.
Now I’m not so sure.
Sometimes a sentence that feels a little too human is exactly the one people remember.
Sometimes the paragraph that breaks the “rules” is the one that makes someone stop scrolling.
And sometimes the best edit is simply leaving the sentence alone.
Not because it’s flawless.
Because it sounds like something an actual person would say.
One Day, Imperfection Might Become a Feature
Imagine opening two articles.
The first is perfectly structured.
Every paragraph flows into the next.
Every sentence is polished.
Every word feels predictable.
The second has a few rough edges.
A sentence runs a little long.
An unexpected story appears in the middle.
The writer changes their mind halfway through and tells you why.
Nothing feels manufactured.
Everything feels lived.
Which one would you finish?
I already know my answer.
And I have a feeling more people will make the same choice over the next few years.
Not because they dislike AI.
I certainly don’t.
I use it every day.
But the easier it becomes to generate flawless content…
…the more we’ll value the little fingerprints that remind us a human was behind the keyboard.
Those fingerprints may soon become the rarest thing on the internet.
And, because of that, the most valuable.
